I think I'm almost done with this issue.  In addition to the unit change mentioned in this issue's title, I've reworked the way chromatic objects are amplitude normalized so that they obey the following invariant (comment extracted from chromatic.py):

```
    # ChromaticObjects should adhere to the following invariants:
    # - Objects should define the attributes:
    #   * .SED, ._norm, .separable, .wave_list, .interpolated
    # - Exactly one of the attributes .SED and ._norm should be None.
    #   * If ._norm is not None, then:
    #     - ._norm should be one of:
    #       * a callable function of wavelength, returning a float normalization
    #       * a scalar float normalization
    #     - obj.evaluateAtWavelength(lam).drawImage().array.sum() == obj._norm(lam)  (callable)
    #     - obj.evaluateAtWavelength(lam).drawImage().array.sum() == obj._norm  (scalar)
    #     - obj.evaluateAtWavelength(lam).drawImage().array.sum() ==
    #       obj.evaluateAtWavelength(lam).getFlux()  # Like all GSObjects
    #     - obj.drawImage(bandpass) raises a ValueError
    #     - Note that ._norm is essentially unitless in this case, and the units of the GSObject
    #       returned by evaluateAtWavelength are 1/arcsec^2 (or 1/scale_unit^2 if not using arcsec
    #       as the implicit angle units).
    #   * If .SED is not None, then:
    #     - .SED should be a galsim.SED instance
    #     - obj.SED.calculateFlux(bandpass) == obj.calculateFlux(bandpass) ==
    #       obj.drawImage(bandpass).array.sum()
    #     - obj.evaluateAtWavelength(lam).drawImage().array.sum() == obj.SED(lam)
    # - .separable is a boolean indicating whether or not the profile can be factored into a
    #   spatial part and a spectral part.
    # - .wave_list is a numpy array indicating wavelengths of particular interest, for instance, the
    #   wavelengths at which SED is explicitly defined via a LookupTable.  These are the wavelengths
    #   that will be used (in addition to those in bandpass.wave_list) when drawing an image of the
    #   chromatic profile.
```

I have a few more ideas that may or may not be worth pursuing including:

- Deprecate `galsim.Chromatic` since `galsim.ChromaticTransformation` can now accomplish the same thing.
- Deprecate `galsim.ChromaticObject.__init__()`, i.e., keep `ChromaticObject` as an abstract base class, but deprecate its direct instantiation.  Instantiating this class is currently only needed just prior to applying a wavelength-dependent transformation to a `GSObject`.  I think it would be more natural, however, for the `GSObject` transformation methods to just accept wavelength-dependent arguments and construct `ChromaticTransformation` objects directly.
- Create a `ChromaticNormalization` type, which `SED` could inherit from.  The difference between `ChromaticNormalization` and `SED` would be that the former is unit-less and the latter has units of photons/nm/cm^2/s.  `ChromaticNormalization` could then replace the `lambda`s I'm currently using to track non-SED wavelength-dependent normalizations, which _might_ lead to nicer looking, better picklable code.
- Add a `size_fac` kwarg to `InterpolatedChromaticObject.__init__()`, to adjust the size of the stored images being interpolated.  I think some of the loss of flux accuracy we see in `InterpolatedChromaticObject` is just due to flux leaking past the edge of the stored images.  This new kwarg would be a more convenient way to recover this flux than manipulating the object-to-be-interpolated's `gsparams.folding_threshold`.

Thoughts?

I retraced the paths you took, and I'm 95% sure I know what happened.

Check `/api/v1/config/` in your system's current state, and then `project_local_paths` out of that. You will probably find that `first-project` is not in the list.

Tower excludes all paths that are already being used by another project. So the likely story that happened is that you first created the project in the UI and _then_ tried to create it in tower-cli. The same would have happened in reverse, except that the UI wouldn't have shown the option in the dropdown.

I agree this error message is frustrating, given the circumstances. I'll forward that on for consideration as a Tower improvement. I'm fairly sure nothing about this is unique to tower-cli, so I'll close this issue.

>This would only be a problem if you move nodes super often and don't have any automation surrounding this process. Also, when you have the ability to provide multiple addresses, it doesn't necessarily mean you have too. If you decide that it's too much work to keep the configuration updated using 3 addresses, just add one. I don't think it makes sense to force infrastructure decisions here.

If you move nodes only infrequently and has an automation in place to change the configuration file, then what's the actual problem we are trying to solve? The issue may happen only if you moved the Etcd node and started a new Patroni cluster after you did so but before the configuration has been changed, presumably a short interval of time. That may happen if you are running something like a DB as a service at a scale with a single template for Patroni that contains your Etcd address, but for that use case one can maintain at least a list A records with the same name pointing to different Etcd addresses, that would already be much better that a list of addresses in the Patroni configuration file.

So I'm a bit concerned about the possible impact of comparing every config value with the DCS tree every single HA loop.  Imagine that someone has their HA poll interval set to 3 seconds because of SLA requirements.  What about having a timestamp instead?  That is, under the config tree, we have a "config_last_updated" timestamp, and we only apply & reload if the local cached timestamp is lower?

    option httpchk GET /replica

Hi @GDavisSS,

To answer your questions:

**Do I have to create a new chalice folder with its own app.py for each trigger or can I somehow capture those triggers in the existing app.py?**
You do not have to create a new chalice application. However, you will have to do some configuration outside of `chalice` to hook up the cognito triggers. A good example of this is in a sample chalice trivia application that I have made. In it, I have two cognito lambda functions that I made using the `app.lambda_function()`: https://github.com/kyleknap/chalice-trivia/blob/3c23543c511db10e1b2a60e306daec2c1079d998/app.py#L87-L98. From there, you can do a `chalice deploy` to get the lambda functions up and running. However, you will need to use the console or the API directly to hook up the deployed lambda functions to the appropriate triggers on your cognito user pool. The good news is that this is only a one time thing. You do not have to do it for each new deployment.

**One last thing, is it possible to have the same trigger for all stages in my api?**
So for each chalice stage, there will be a separate lambda function created and I believe for cognito triggers you can only have one lambda function per type of trigger. So you would either have to:

1) Choose a lambda function from a particular stage (probably from a production stage) and use that for trigger.
2) Create a user pool for each chalice stage and associate the appropriate lambda functions of that stage to the associated user pool for that stage.

I would recommend doing option 2. However in general, I feel that it would be nice if chalice managed the creation of cognito user pools and triggers per stage and would be a good candidate in this proposal for managed AWS resources: https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/516

Let us know if that helps.

Summary of discussion in chat regarding locks for posterity

- no calls to `publish` need to be wrapped with a `self._lock` (the fact that `will_fork_state` publish above did this was a bug)
- all callbacks execute in separate worker threads, so there is only process unsafety considerations if they access shared data. shared data refers to the global executor context (Executor._shared_context, accessed through Executor.locked_context` (see executor._fork_state_callback for an example)
- if a callback accessed global shared data, it is the callback's responsibility to use the aforementioned lock when accessing it
- currently only callbacks at the executor level even have access to the shared data. a deeper callback at the state, platform, cpu level for example could not even do process unsafe things if it wanted to

I was hoping for a second unbiased opinion or some compromise to tie-break the unresolved "options design" stalemate in this PR, but assuming that isn't going to materialize I think we should figure out a path forward in the name of progress as this seems to be a pain point for several users.

My view is still that overloading `--platform` here with an alternate extended form is the most ideal from a UX and integration standpoint. pex != pip, so we really shouldn't be striving to exactly replicate pip's UI - but instead do what makes the most sense for the project itself and it's consumers. I've actually never been a fan of the options split/ergonomics as used by `pip` which this PR gleans from and would be less than thrilled about forklifting that directly into pex.

With that said, I had eagerly landed #394 to improve the pex + bazel integration story while also hoping to force the issue a bit here so that the union of these two changes would make the design challenge more apparent. Since this was my doing, I'd be happy to do the remaining work to carry this PR over the finish line if we can just agree on a strategy for that.

So in the interest of progress, how would you feel about starting with an overloaded form of `--platform` as the primary interface to this change? Then, if after some experimentation we decide that that API is just too ergonomically terrible for whatever reason, we can revisit adding the single-shot options split as a separate PR?

If that works, I could likely get something out in the next couple of days.

> 3.6. Distribution of Executable Versions.
> You may distribute Covered Code in Executable form only if the requirements of Section 3.1-3.5 have been met for that Covered Code, ...

Meaning that any changes that we make to the freeimage source code must be made available under the same license. But we make no changes to freeimage itself.

> ... and if You include a notice stating that the Source Code version of the Covered Code is available under the terms of this License, including a description of how and where You have fulfilled the obligations of Section 3.2. The notice must be conspicuously included in any notice in an Executable version, related documentation or collateral in which You
describe recipients' rights relating to the Covered Code. 

So, we should mention/provide the FreeImage license in our docs and let our users know that any derived work should also mention the FI license.

> You may distribute the Executable version of Covered Code under a license of Your choice, which may contain terms different from this License,
provided that You are in compliance with the terms of this License and that the license for the Executable version does not attempt to limit or alter the recipient's rights in the Source Code version from the rights set forth in this License. You hereby agree to indemnify the Initial Developer and every Contributor for any liability incurred by the Initial Developer or such Contributor as a result of any such terms You offer.

IIUC this means we can ship and use the library, and as long as we honor the FI license for the FI code, we can use whatever license we want for *our* code.

You appear to be using incorrect Python syntax for passing extra (PMML)Pipeline arguments. The correct syntax would be:
```Python
iris_pipeline = PMMLPipeline([
  ("classifier", LGBMClassifier())
])
iris_pipeline.fit(iris_df, iris.target, classifier__categorical_feature = ["petal length (cm)"])
```

Very likely cannot be implemented as a meta-estimator class, because verification data is presented in the "original" form, whereas estimators make predictions based on the "transformed" form (eg. all categorical features must have been one-hot-encoded).

The simplest and most intuitive way seems to be adding a method `add_verification_data(X, precision = 1e-13, zeroThreshold = 1e-13)` to the `sklearn2pmml.PMMLPipeline` class. The pipeline can then "use itself" to make predictions, and store them with the `X` data matrix.

Hi @muammar. when you play a uri, Sonos will treat it as a file / track or a stream based on its prefix. Prefixes `http & https` default to file / track. Often you may want these to be a stream / radio so switching the prefix to ` x-rincon-mp3radio` Sonos will treat it as a stream. We have updated SoCo to allow this choice in #443 and #491 there is also documentation in the later - these will be in the next release. 

This should apply to all formats Sonos support. This page shows supported formats: [https://sonos.custhelp.com/app/answers/detail/a_id/80/~/supported-audio-formats](url)

I have been unable to find a `.wav` stream to test this on - if you have one please let me try it.
Hope this helps

In Summary this PR does two things:

1) Adds features that help with plugins and modules such as:
* Provides a mechanism for Plugins to register their own URLs modules to the Core (appropriately namespaced).
* Provides a trigger method on the Base `KolibriModule` class so that KolibriModules can easily trigger events on the Mediator.
* Adds a frontend asset block tag to the base.html to allow Django templates that extend it can add more frontend assets.

2) Provides a skeleton Learn app with the following features:
* Shows three buttons representing content nodes of different types.
* Each content node button triggers a different kind of 'content_render' event specific to a content mimetype.
* When the button is pressed, the appropriate event is triggered.
* In addition, three content renderer plugins are defined, each of which listens to a specific mimetype.
* These plugins are all asynchronously registered, so are not in the page at page load.
* Once a button has been pressed, the appropriate plugin receives data about the event, and a reference for where to render its content.
* The plugin renders its content into the element provided.

What it does not do:
* Clear up the DOM, it just keeps appending nodes.
* Do any kind of URL routing, so it is unaware if it is already showing the current content node.
* Interface with the content app at all to actually receive metadata about content, these are just hardcoded.

> My opinion is that running all the tests for every PR is not bloat, but is rather using a test server precisely as intended. If anything, we should run a limited set of tests locally before pushing (if the test suit gets large enough to warrant it) and let the test server do the heavy work.

I really agree with this, it's a matter of adjusting it with very explicit exceptions. And if we cannot identify an exception to the rule (_test everything_), then we _test everything_ :)

Reality has been quite cruel.. for KA Lite. Even though we tested stuff locally to avoid waiting 20-40 minutes for the CI, a lot of tears were spilled over shaky test frameworks.. because they caused communication work regarding false negatives.. and some traumatizing debugging :) First case of this same phenomena in Kolibri is the sphinx link checker.. which IMO is really useful but should only be executed when explicitly asking for a full set of tests or when changing .rst files.

Btw. I maintain an open source project in which only some of the tests were running but all lights were green... when we finally fixed the test discoverer, we had at least a couple severe issues :) -> https://github.com/django-money/django-money/pull/162/files ...with that said, I'm alert to putting _too much_ complexity into the testing frameworks in case should be unknowing that our software has become Real Media Player because our test framework says otherwise :)

Hi @aanand  - The reason relates to https://github.com/docker/docker/pull/14530 and mentioned in https://github.com/docker/docker-py/pull/691. The daemon with 14530 will do network mode validation - previously it was done by the CLI. This is necessary as part of the port of the daemon to Windows where network modes are different between Linux and Windows. Hence, a more complete host config is necessary to be passed to the daemon. Strictly speaking, it probably should have been there before as many of the tests aren't passing a "complete" host config structure, but instead are passing the minimal set to allow the docker-py tests to pass. It's great to hear that trackpy works for you! (Except for this.)

Your tiff stack is larger than 2 GB, which means that 32 bits is not enough to address every location in the file. Somewhere there is a 32-bit component that must be 64 bits instead. The first thing to check is whether your version of Python is a 32 or 64-bit version. Based on the error message it sounds like that is the problem.

If Windows and Python are both 64 bits, then there could be a bug in pims or one of its dependencies. You will need to create an issue at the pims repository.

The workaround is very easy: convert your tiff stack into a directory of many regular tiff files. But obviously that is not ideal.

> It might also make sense to add original owner as a collaborator while transferring, but I wanted some opinions on that.

I had mixed feelings on this and here's the best argument I could come up with in its favor:

When you transfer a repository in github or bitbucket, the receiver needs to _accept_ the transfer, but our workflows don't currently support that sort of transaction.  This creates the potential for an interim period without an "active" owner.

Q:  What happens if the new owner is on vacation and something immediately goes wrong, necessitating a release?

A: An admin could intervene.

If I'm defeating my own strongest argument in its favor, I'm narrowly against it, but I'd really like to hear some other opinions on this.

What I've deduced thusfar is that argument parsing is _succeeding_ for you when it shouldn't.
You're giving argparse a command which has (in argparse-speak) subparsers attached to it, but not supplying a subcommand to match one of those subparsers.

The fact that this is a usage error is something that was broken and then fixed during Python 3.3 development: http://bugs.python.org/issue9253#msg186387

I'm going to fix this by manually setting the `required` attribute on the subparser object, which should be compatible with all relevant pythons, and fix it for the pythons where it is "incorrect" per argparse documentation.

Great comments, @ukoethe. It turns out I've gone down a bit of a rabbit hole here with regards to this fix. This is _good news_, in the sense that I'm seeing a _lot_ of simplification in the code, which in turn is further testimony that treating features like dependencies is the right choice. Furthermore, moving ahead, when we consider additional functionality in conda like `namespace` and `provides` constructs, the proper behavior is going to be easier to reason about.

This where I am converging right now. Features have one key difference compared to dependencies: features should not be installed unless they _have_ to be. For instance, if you install `python`, you know you're going to be selecting either `vc9`, `vc10`, or `vc14`. If you install `mkl`, you know you'll be getting the `mkl` track feature because its a metapackage created for that specific purpose. But if I _don't_ install `mkl` (or `accelerate`) it shouldn't select any of the packages that have the `mkl` feature.

What this has led to is the following _three-stage_ optimization pass in the solver when in "install" mode. 

- First, minimize the number of installed features in the solution.
- Second, maximize the version numbers of all requested packages.
- Third, minimize the number of dependencies installed.

At first this sounds expensive, but in fact, thanks to some concomitant solver improvements I've implemented, it's working great. "Update" and "remove" modes have similar multi-pass programs, but they get to use the same multi-pass optimization infrastructure. We'll be able to implement some really good policies here.

So bear with me, you're going to see a few more major commits on this PR before it's ready to merge, but I think we're all going to benefit from it.

* "*conda environments*"
* "*conda env*" specifications (different from a "*conda environment*")

* `conda env`
* `anaconda`
* `anaconda-project`

It is not really supported in python to use utf-8 encoded data in `str` when having system encoding set to `ascii`. The change we made only uncovers the problem - previously we were masking it, and it would break very unpredictably if other encoding that utf-8 were used.

The solution is to use `unicode` when working with non-ascii data or set your encoding properly. Or use Python 3.

I will add it into the Changelog to document the change.

TimeService falling behind is not load dependent.

loop_time() (which is what I'm guessing is used for timeouts) will never fall behind by more than about 1 second.

time() and strtime() will fall behind, no matter what the load is.  strtime() falling behind causes Date headers in responses to be incorrect (this is how I discovered this).

The sandboxing is so that HTML files you view cannot talk to kernels the server is running - it should mean that cross-domain protection kicks in between the content in the iframe and the notebook server.

We do sandbox it [with the allow-scripts flag](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/d00b7e3e17274bd879be4c23012ce5f6fbce896a/notebook/templates/view.html#L26), but maybe browsers aren't respecting that?

We should only need the sandboxing for files that can run code in the browser, like html and svg (?), so you can workaround this for a PDF by changing `/view/` to `/files/` in the URL. But PDF.js is probably complex enough that I'd have some reservations about letting it out of the sandbox by default, in case a malicious PDF can cause it to run Javascript and then talk to kernels.

We just did some quick experiments and it seems that calling `import gc; gc.collect()` explicitly does seem to prevent prevent memory from building up.

Also, we think we identified the problem: originally, these lines in `backward_pass`

```python
while tape:
    node = tape.pop()
    ...
```

ensured that there were no more circular dependencies when the backward pass finished by popping things off the tape, so that reference counting would be sufficient to clean up garbage. However, to support efficient jacobians I added this line:

```python
tape = copy.copy(tape)  # <-- problem!
while tape:
    node = tape.pop()
    ...
```

which had the unintended result of preserving circular dependencies and hence preventing reference counting cleanup.

Still, after `tape` goes out of scope (when `grad` returns), `gc.collect()` should be able to garbage collect these things via `gc.collect()`, and so adding explicit calls to `gc.collect()` to user-level code after calling `grad` should work. (We could also add it to the definition of `grad`.)

We're going to implement a fix (and probably eliminate tapes in the long term), but for now if you're having memory problems try calling `gc.collect()` in your code after computing a gradient.

So naming aside, I went ahead and added some higher level logic to sort of illustrate why I think a object map is powerful:

https://github.com/nginxinc/crossplane/blob/636ea85305ded3b421bd05c94b5e3823700decc2/tests/test_objects.py#L40-L52

These new asserts use these new primatives:

https://github.com/nginxinc/crossplane/blob/636ea85305ded3b421bd05c94b5e3823700decc2/crossplane/objects.py#L55-L102

In addition to the previously added `__contains__` magic method.  But basically the summary is as follows:

*property - file:* Will recursively walk the object tree and find the NGXConfigFile object the NGXDirective belongs to and return it.

*property - location:* Will return the file name and line number within that file of the current directive which is useful for user messaging (e.g. NGINX Amplify Static Analysis reports).

*method - context:* Will return the NGINX obeyed context for a set of directives.  This is _super_ powerful feature that was previously known as `_find_cascading_directives` in NGINX Amplify back end code.  This object/crossplane structure makes this 1000x simpler.

At any rate, these are just illustrations of low hanging fruit.  There is a lot we can do on top of this layer.

Ok, I thought we had discussed this and you said never have transitive dependencies in case they change — I guess since we're the ones writing both packages we'll know BPL will never _not_ be a Toil dependency.

Yes, there already are other things in `/mnt/ephemeral` and HDFS should operate within a subdirectory thereof.

I am ok with the workaround of creating `/mnt/ephemeral/hdfs` inside the container for now. In the container it would be `/hdfs/hdfs`, so you may want to consider `-v /mnt/ephemeral/:/ephemeral/:rw` or even `-v /mnt/ephemeral/:/mnt/ephemeral/:rw` to make it less confusing.

In the long run there is `/var/lib/toil` which is bind-mounted by cgcloud from `/mnt/persistent/var/lib/toil`.  Most scripts in toil-scripts set the working dir to that directory (via Toil's `--workDir` flag). You can then use `job.fileStore.getLocalTempDir()` within a job to get a job-specific temporary directory somewhere underneath `--workDir`. The catch is that currently the bind mount is hardwired to the persistent volume, not the ephemeral one which is what you would want. I think that is a mistake and it can easily be changed. In

https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/cgcloud/blob/master/toil/src/cgcloud/toil/toil_box.py#L77

`persistent=True` should be `persistent=False`. I will make that change now. It's up to you if you want to upgrade cgcloud and create a new toil-box image or if you want to go ahead with the hack.

Ok, for me it was enough to simply make the change I mentioned above.

Through trial and error I found the XBuild is null-ref'ing when processing this
https://github.com/dotnet/standard/blob/d2b6c46ce1fa1d7d76a7f8f34b96da1d1053583e/netstandard/pkg/targets/NETStandard.Library.targets#L12 and https://github.com/dotnet/standard/blob/d2b6c46ce1fa1d7d76a7f8f34b96da1d1053583e/netstandard/pkg/targets/NETStandard.Library.targets#L18

It should even be processing those since the _NetStandardLibraryLibPath and _NetStandardLibraryRefPath should not be set for desktop projects and those items will be empty.  For my repro it was enough to duplicate the conditions on the root ItemGroup.

So the whole file looks like:
```xml
<Project xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/developer/msbuild/2003">
  <PropertyGroup>

    <ReferenceCopyLocalPaths Condition="'$(_NetStandardLibraryLibPath)' != ''" Include="$(_NetStandardLibraryLibPath)*.dll">

Powerline does not put any restrictions on which modules exactly need to have the matchers and segments, so you could put it in *any* module, not necessary `powerline.matchers.vim.plugin.ctrlp` and `powerline.segments.vim.plugin.ctlp`. Including putting both matcher and segment in one module.

These are *not* configuration files, so you *should not* put them into `.config/powerline`. You can’t do this thing with modules named `powerline.*`, but if your module was named e.g. `powerline_ctrlp` you could put it anywhere in `sys.path`. `sys.path` may be modified by setting `PYTHONPATH`, it is usual colon-separated environment variable listing directories like `$PATH` is.

With application keys restricted to a single bucket, you are not allowed to call `b2_list_buckets` without specifying the name or ID of the one bucket the key is allowed to use.

When you call `b2_authorize_account`, it returns a new field called `allowed`, with the list of things the key is allowed to do, and the ID if the bucket it's restricted to (if there is a restriction).  When there is a bucket restriction, I'm thinking it would be a good idea to go ahead and get the details of that bucket and store them with the account info.  Then, when a command needs a bucket, it can check whether it's the bucket that is allowed, and print a helpful error message if not.  (For security reasons, the service just returns "not_authorized" if you try to access the wrong bucket.)

When dump_only is True, shouldn't this field be omitted completely from the spec as an parameter argument?

The Meta field will be inherited like any other attribute. If it's not present, look for it in the MRO.  If it is present, then use that value instead.

If you need to inherit some of the parent's configuration and override some, use inheritance on the Meta. 

```python
class ParentSchema(Schema):
    class Meta:
        strict = True
```

Now any schema that inherits from this schema and doesn't provide its own Meta will be strict.

```python
class ChildSchema(ParentSchema):
    class Meta(ParentSchema.Meta):
        ordered = True
``` 

This schema is both strict and ordered because it explicitly inherits the parent's Meta class and added new attributes  to it, similar to inheriting a regular class and adding our overriding attributes.

As a note, `ordered` looks like it's "infectious." If *any* parent schema is ordered, yours is too even if you set `ordered=False`. I'm unaware of the motivations for this so I can't say if it's a bug or not but be aware.

(1) Reshaping. The reshape layers in CoreML requires the total number of elements does not change. This means `np.prod(np.array(input_shape)) == np.prod(np.array(output_shape))`. In your case, height is reduced from 6 to 5, so reshaping layer cannot handle this case. 
(2) CoreML's upsampling layer and downsampling layer currently restricts the scaling factors in be integers. 
(3) As a not-so-cool workaround, you might try upsampling your blob by 5 then downsampling it by 6.

Thanks for your input.

> > Replaces the file with a copy
> > all file system changes will be flushed to disk.
>
> these are implementation details and shouldn't be in the API. atomicity is the key property here. implementation strategy and durability should not be specified.

I didn't want to be too specific as in reality the drive/file system can lie about flushing and on Windows we don't know what's happening exactly. We could say it does an atomic replace if the OS/hardware supports it, if not it will at least reduce the probability of data corruption as far as possible (?)

> > def replace(self, filename=None)
>
> IMHO this is the wrong API. no file system in real use that i know of is fully transactional. this API provides and abstraction that is not well supported underneath. in the end, it will create a copy of the file in all supported OSes; this is why i think it is the wrong API.

Why is it wrong?

> a more useful API would be saveAs(self, file=None) that saves pending changes to a copy of the file somewhere else. API help would include a note that, if 'file' parameter is missing, it will save to a temp file and then replace the original as atomically as possible. (and stating that this saving mode is safer and slower than the usual save is OK; but it should be noted that the usual save is safe enough for normal use.)

There might be a misunderstanding here. I planed exactly what you propose with replace().
The filename argument allows to save tags to another file than loaded from, like``ID3().replace("foo.mp3")``. Imo temp file creation should be an implementation detail as the only logical place to put it in is the same directory. Or can you think of a use case providing your own temp file?

> > This operation can be significantly slower than save().
> 
> yes, the saveAs() API could be added. but the important issue here is that it doesn't fix the problem with the current implementation of save()! furthermore, you leave it to the app programmer to decide what to do, and they know little or nothing about risks. what do you expect them to do? wouldn't all of them either choose replace()/saveAs() or ask you a question personally?

Good question. I'll have to think about that.

> plus the 'safe save' is only needed when the ID3v2 tag is added or resized, because loosing the tag is not as bad a loosing the payload, and the tag is so small that a partial save is really unlikely. and with replace/saveAs you force a copy on each save, when 99% of the time it is not needed.

I don't like multiple shades of "safe". If we somehow leave the file in an invalid but playable state, the next tagger might get tricked into corrupting the file, or some players wont accept the file. And I really don't want to think at every operation in the code about what would happen if there was a crash right after. And is there any guarantee that file operations get flushed to disk in the order I execute them?

> what is really needed is a safe 'payload scroll' only when resizing the ID3v2 tag. IMO, the normal save should be upgraded to behave in a **safe enough** manner, and that is it. this entails copying the file but only when a payload move is needed. more free space is needed by the new save? too bad. i rather have a reasonably safe save that complains the disk is full than a dangerous save. if the disk is full, users will have to deal with that very soon, so let them deal with it now.

Yes, the `echo` issue is a tricky. The problem is that Z3 had that command before it became an official SMT command and some applications use it to synchronize their Z3 output parsers, and if the strings don't match they will just keep waiting for the right string forever. We could add an additional command `echoq` or something like that, but I don't really like that either. And, the other way round, we can't really throw a useful error or deprecation message because we don't know what applications expect (except if smtlib2_compliant=true). I'd be happy to change the default behavior at the next major (4.6.?) release, but we would have to find a way to notify applications and developers about this change in some way.

Ok, I will reproduce the same plots for my experience, but at this stage I'm pretty confident there is an issue somewhere.

> If you made Layer._get_output private and steer people towards a public API for get_output on multiple layers, seems that would be enough without global memoization too?

Yes, but then `Layer._get_output()` would need to pass around those dictionaries that hold the output expressions of all other layers. This would be harder to understand than the iterative approach of the `get_output` function. The only possible advantage would be that `_get_output()` could be redefined in subclasses, but I don't see the use case yet.

> I am not proud of it, but apparently I was using `get_output` exactly in the way you wanted to prevent.

We're sorry :) The documentation tells you to avoid it, but of course hardly anybody will read the full docstring because the usage of `get_output` seems clear. We should probably try to extend the tutorial / library walkthrough with networks of multiple input or multiple output layers.

> One Layer does not need to know what was the input to the InputLayer and which Layers came before it, it just needs to know if it has a symbolic output in its cache for its current symbolic inputs and tags

Right, it could be something like:
```python
class Layer(object):
    def get_output(self, inputs, **kwargs):
        if isinstance(inputs, dict) and self in inputs:
            return inputs[self]
        inputs = self.input_layer.get_output(inputs, **kwargs)
        if (inputs, kwargs) not in self._cache:
            self._cache[(inputs, kwargs)] = self.get_output_for(inputs, **kwargs)
        return self._cache[(inputs, kwargs)]
```
The problem is not that each layer would have to cache a lot, but that the layer instances as a whole would hold a lot of memoized expressions that include shared variables taking up GPU memory. Those are the shared variables the Layers hold themselves anyway, but we'd run into the same problems as in Layer.params (#310, still unfixed) -- they wouldn't be freed when deleting the layers, since they'd become dictionary keys. The only solution we saw when discussing #104 was to make the cache keys weak references to the input variables (the `inputs` passed to `get_output`, not the outputs of the previous layer), so the cache would disappear at least if the user discards all references to an input variable. But it all didn't seem satisfactory, so we went the global helper function route.

> I struggled a lot to achieve the same, which is why I opened this issue!

What exactly was it you're trying to achieve? Are you trying to create a layer that wraps an arbitrary subgraph? The CustomRecurrentLayer does that for its input-to-hidden connection. Your implementation doesn't look correct, are you sure about your `get_output_for()`?

> Is this meant to be a more 'lightweight' alternative?

Ideally, yes. It should avoid taking too much out of the users' hands, or at least also offer something that's between Lasagne's MNIST example (all the boilerplate code) and nolearn (no boilerplate code), to make it more obvious how to customize things (in contrast to Keras). That's also why I'd like to avoid a model or trainer class if possible.

> Any one has some suggestions ?

The best (most performant) way to implement a siamese network is to make your batches twice as large (concatenating the examples that'd feed into the left and right leg) and splitting or reshaping them when they reach the head (with a SliceLayer to obtain the left and right part, or a ReshapeLayer to rearrange the tensors such that the left and right parts are concatenated). If you do this, the BN layer will normalize values using all the inputs simultaneously. Otherwise, the only way is to write a custom BN layer that accepts and normalizes multiple inputs.

My logic is that we want things like `GangaUnitTest` to be accessible from anywhere in any Ganga plugin with a simple `import`. Putting these things into a `GangaTesting` plugin means that that plugin must be loaded explicitly before the tests are runnable.

There are two separate things in the test system: 

1. Test framework stuff like `GangaUnitTest`, `run_until_state` etc. I'd like these to be globally accessible so I think in the long term having them in `Ganga.tests.lib` is sensible (we could call it `Ganga.tests.framework` or whatever).
2. The tests themselves. We should make sure that tests for a particular plugin live in that plugin so that they are as near to the relevant code as possible. This means having core tests in `Ganga/tests` (currently `Ganga/new_tests`) and Dirac tests in `GangaDirac/tests`.

In general I don't like putting things in `GangaTest` or similar since I feel it is abusing the plugin system somewhat to just add things to `sys.path`.

As for renaming `Ganga/new_tests` to `Ganga/tests`, I think that will have to wait until we are ready to switch off the legacy tests (unless we can change them to look in `Ganga/old_tests` or similar?)

Very first version of an hosts group status:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2932687/18349904/ae8578c2-75d3-11e6-903d-21255a551672.png)

The hosts group status is computed as the worst real state of the hosts members of the group.

The real state of an host is:
 - `ok` if the host is UP and all its services are OK
 - `warning` if the host is UNREACHABLE or at least one of its services is WARNING
 - critical` if the host is DOWN or at least one of its services is CRITICAL
 - `acknowledged' if the host is acknowledged or at least one of its services is acknowledged and no other services are WARNING or CRITICAL
 - `downtime' if the host is in a scheduled downtime or at least one of its services is in a scheduled downtime and no other services are WARNING or CRITICAL

Hi @mattswoboda -- sorry for all your troubles! In general, debugging our cameras comes down to 2 or 3 things. Very rarely it's a problem with the software in this repo, and even more rarely is it a H/W issue. 

  1. Make sure it's on a compatible system (where compatibility is a function of supported USB host controller chipset that provides enough power)
  2. Driver installation issues (In 99% of cases this means Ubuntu/Linux)
  3. Firmware. We don't track DCM version number (... and that ecosystem is largely a disaster because of all the reasons you mention related to the installation/upgrade process).

Please shoot me an email @ dimitri.diakopoulos@intel.com and I'll send you the direct F/W update binary for Windows. That'll be a good place to start. There definitely should be a mechanism to uninstall the DCM, so definitely do that as well!

## Objetive
Get the depth image and the color image in two separate folder.

## Code
```
import argparse

import pyrealsense2 as rs
import numpy as np
import cv2
import os


LOAD_BAG = True
SAVE_DEPTH = True
SAVE_RGB = True


def main():
    if not os.path.exists(args.directory):
        os.mkdir(args.directory)
    try:
        config = rs.config()
        if LOAD_BAG:
            rs.config.enable_device_from_file(config, args.input, False)
        pipeline = rs.pipeline()
        if SAVE_DEPTH:
            config.enable_stream(rs.stream.depth, 1280, 720, rs.format.z16, 30)
        if SAVE_RGB:
            config.enable_stream(rs.stream.color, 1280, 720, rs.format.rgb8, 30)
        pipeline.start(config)
        i = 0
        while True:
            print("Saving frame:", i)
            frames = pipeline.wait_for_frames()
            if SAVE_DEPTH:
                depth_frame =  frames.get_depth_frame()
                depth_image = np.asanyarray(depth_frame.get_data())
                depth_image[np.where((depth_image > 3000))] = 0
                cv2.imwrite(args.directory + "/depth/" + str(i).zfill(6) + ".png", depth_image)
            if SAVE_RGB:
                color_frame = frames.get_color_frame()
                color_image = np.asanyarray(color_frame.get_data())
                cv2.imwrite(args.directory + "/images/" + str(i).zfill(6) + ".png", color_image)
            i += 1
    except RuntimeError:
        print("No more frames arrived, reached end of BAG file!")

    finally:
        pass


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("-d", "--directory", type=str, help="Path to save the images")
    parser.add_argument("-i", "--input", type=str, help="Bag file to read")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    main()
r

@ddiakopoulos  I tested another camera, same effect, but not as strong. I'm wondering: is the intrinsic calibration performed by Intel or by creative ? During the assembling process by creative, the camera might be exposed to (minimal) pressure. 

A bended circuit leads to a shift (alpha) of the projected patterns. The camera does calculate the depth information based on the location of each pattern in the image. As the pattern position shifts, the camera assumes a wrong distance ( crossing between camera ray and the expected ray for a given pattern).

![img_0022](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/16840133/12612136/ef6f8592-c4f0-11e5-97ff-33d57a55dc2f.JPG)

You may correct me if I'm wrong but I believe this leads to the blending effect.

In order to use this technology for scientific projects it might be useful to have a method to correct this effect. (I'm unsure about the SR300 solving this problem entirely, as it seems to use the same technology )

1. The middleware cant put the user in context, it supposed to get it as input. There are 2 ways to do it:
1.1. Set it in the handler before executing the query
1.2. Instead of using the `context` set it in the middleware's constructor when you execute the query:
```
class AuthorizationMiddleware(object):
  def __init__(self, user):
    self.user = user

  def resolve(self, next_step, root, args, context, info):
    ... do authorization logic ...

                                variable_values=variables,

Old API:

```python
class CustomManager(models.Model):
    use_for_related_fields = True

class Model(models.Model):
    custom_manager = CustomManager()
```

New API:

```python
class Model(models.Model):
    custom_manager = CustomManager()

    class Meta:
        base_manager_name = 'custom_manager'
````

I'm implementing a general spinel property getter function with prototype like `GetProperty(spinel_key_t aKey, const char *aFormat, ...)`.
The caller assumes all data are properly assigned when the function returns successfully. Instead of writing parsing code for each property, I reuse the `spinel_datatype_vunpack*()` API to implement a simple general property handler for all properties with the supplied `aFormat` as the `pack_format` argument of `spinel_datatype_vunpack*()`. Since composite data like string returned from `spinel_datatype_vunpack()` are actually located in the HDLC frame, which are only valid during the HDLC decoder's frame callback. Once `GetProperty(spinel_key_t aKey, const char *aFormat, ...)` returned, these data already became invalid. So in this PR I implemented a alternative way for parsing the spinel data so that composite data are copied into the caller supplied buffers.

As for the symmetry design, it's really beautiful and I like it. And I also noticed that `scanf()` and `printf()` are very similar to spinel's `*unpack()` and `*pack()`, reader and writer. The reader `scanf()` just like `*unpack()` accepts a pointer to the type that would be passed in to the writer `printf()`, while for string both `scanf()` and `printf()` accepts a pointer to `char` and the format code is the same `%s`. I didn't feel confused, IMO, maybe because in C, a parameter is passed by pointer if,
1. the parameter is going to be changed by the function,
2. or the parameter is composite data types and passing by pointer is more efficient by value.
So maybe, it's acceptable for composite data type that they are not that symmetry in reader and writer, i.e. `*unpack()` and `*pack()`.

As for the performance concern, this PR didn't change the existing API, so the caller only use the in place parsing API if needed. Besides, to enable making copy after unpacking as suggested, that lead to providing specified parsing code for each spinel property, which may result in a lot of repeated code.

Hi @omerarshad,

1. In supervised mode, fastText learns embeddings for word n-grams. In order to bound the memory used by the model, the word n-grams are hashed into a fixed number of buckets, and there is one embedding for each bucket. Thus, if there are collisions, multiple n-grams will share the same embedding. The number of bucket can be set with the command line option `-bucket`, and is equal to 2M by default. The maximum length of word n-grams can be set with the command line option `-wordNgrams` and is equal to 1 by default.
2. By default, the supervised mode does not use subwords. This can be changed by using the command line options `-minn` and `-maxn` (both are equal to 0 by default), which set the minimum and maximum length of the subwords that are taken into account. A good starting point is probably `-minn 4 -maxn 4`.

Best,
Edouard.

Thanks for pointing this out. I've been doing some research into function signatures in Python 3.x.

TL;DR Bad news: We can't add `__text_signature__` because the format is incompatible with pybind11. Good news: We should be able to add `__signature__` which would accomplish the same goal but also include even more information (type annotations).

There are a couple of issues with `__text_signature__` which make it a bad fit for pybind11. The format [does not support annotations](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/f9169ce6b48c7cc7cc62d9eb5e4ee1ac7066d14b/Lib/inspect.py#L1976) which we use for type information. The default `__text_signature__` is a property which parses the docstring for the [hardcoded signature marker](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/6969eaf4682beb01bc95eeb14f5ce6c01312e297/Objects/typeobject.c#L111) which assumes there is no return type annotation. In contrast, pybind11 generates signatures like: `add(a: int, b: int) -> int`. There's also the fact that `__text_signature__` isn't officially documented which might make it a bit uncertain. Although, the lack of type annotations is already a deal breaker.

Instead, I think we can make use of `__signature__` which is well documented in [PEP 362](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0362/) and available since Python 3.3. This was sort of the plan [before](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/pull/310), but hasn't quite materialized yet. I'll try to put it together now (`__signature__` can be a property which lazily generates the `Signature` object by parsing our docstring format). Implementing this should also resolve issue #990.

Okay, I'm pretty happy with this now. Here's the logic, from top to bottom, new parts in bold:

* If `CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD` is set, do nothing
* **`If PYBIND11_CXX_FEATURES` is set, use those compile features (CMake 3.1+)**
* If `PYBIND11_CPP_STANDARD` is set, adds those flags
* **If CMake < 3.8:** try to auto-detect and fill in `PYBIND11_CPP_STANDARD` with the latest standard flag (11, 14, **17,** specialized MSVC support). Cached.
* **If CMake >= 3.8, automatically fill in `PYBIND11_CXX_FEATURES` with highest supported meta-feature (11, 14, 17). Faster configure time.  Cached.**

I've added a one time message if auto-discovery works, telling the user the mode and the mechanism. It won't display if you set one of these variables yourself or if you are rerunning (already in cache).

This allows pybind11 to take advantage of the faster and officially supported CMake methods for C++ standard discovery (CMake pre-populates the compiler feature list). It also improves interaction with other targets, removes double std listings, and might fix #1097, allowing CMake 3.8+ to mix C++ and C targets (please check).

To test this, you'll need to clear your CMakeCache.txt or remove the value from `PYBIND11_CPP_STANDARD`.

If it looks good, I can add a note to the docs and possibly release notes. The other option would be adding compile features for 3.1-3.8, but those would be a list of features pybind11 needs, and CMake would then pick what flags to pass; the meta-features seems simpler and seems to be the primary way CMake is adding feature support for C++17. The old way of individual features was nice because it could detect if you didn't have support, such as G++ 4.7 vs. 4.8. This method still supports someone adding a list of features to `PYBIND11_CXX_FEATURES`, so this seems to be the most general solution.

Sorry for the cryptic and unexpected test failures here. What's going on is the different pieces packages in this repo are not in required lockstep. For example, you can have a one version of `certbot` and another version of `certbot-apache`. We have tests to make sure that the minimum version we say we require is correct which is failing here because `acme.magic_typing` is new for us.

To fix this, you should modify `certbot-apache/setup.py` to require `acme>0.24.0` and the `acme` line in `certbot-apache/local-oldest-requirements.txt` to `-e acme[dev]`. You can take my word on the latter or I can explain to you how this works if you're curious.

We have an internal `Addr` object that parses ports out of address strings. The port it saves is always an `int`, even though it's being represented as a string. But the port number that comes in from certbot is actually an `int`. We sometimes need to compare these two, and that's where bugs arise. Ideally, ports will be dealt with as `int`s anytime within certbot code, and strings only when they're read in or written out.
e.g., https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/pull/8721#issuecomment-272825141

OK, I'm now sure that the Travis "sync account" button is the source of the trouble here. In particular, if you are a member of the conda-forge organization, then Travis has to look at over 5000 repositories (the rate limit is 5000 requests per user per hour, shared across all oauth applications). 

There's not much we can do about this, aside from improving the messaging. I'm sending a PR that will indicate that the rate limit is hit and how long until it resets. Unfortunately, I suspect Travis also monitors the rate limit and restarts the sync. So it is possible that even after the reset time, the limit will be hit again. I don't have access to 10000 repos, so I can't say for sure (all I could find was [this old issue](https://github.com/travis-ci/travis-ci/issues/837)).

Also, it looks like the request before the OTP code does count as an unauthenticated request as far as rate limits are concerned, at least according to the headers, but it doesn't actually prevent the final authentication with OTP if the remaining unauthenticated requests is 0.

I've made a PR with improved messaging at https://github.com/drdoctr/doctr/pull/320.

I'm not very familiar with the internals of Django's lazy objects, but it seems to me like this could be fixed by defining `__radd__` for the values returned by `ugettext_lazy`.

Minimal case of the error:
```
from django.utils.translation import ugettext_lazy as _
lazy_text = _("abc")
lazy_text + "123"  # works fine
"123" + lazy_text  # TypeError: Can't convert '__proxy__' object to str implicitly
```

...So if `lazy_text.__radd__` was defined, I think this bug would be solved.

I did a quick test to make sure defining `__radd__` lets us add to strings:
```
class A:
    def __radd__(self, x):
        return x + " A"
    def __add__(self, x):
        return "A " + x

a = A()
a+"123"  # "A 123"
"123"+a  # "123 A"   ...success!
```

As far as i understand it. Twitch wants the author of the software that accesses their API to register a Client-ID. They don't demand that every user registers their own Client-ID, they just want a uniq one
per Application.

Livestreamer already has one registered client_id = "ewvlchtxgqq88ru9gmfp1gmyt6h2b93".
The patch by LoneFox78 in the https://github.com/chrippa/livestreamer/issues/1478 comments
already fix the whole issue by sending the already present client_id when requesting the API.

Of course this concept is totally based around commercial software or at least software that's
based around one single developer.

I fully understand if the author of this fork does not want a Twitch account. A possible solution
would be to register a new Twitch account (maybe called streamlink) with as little personal information
as possible. No solution is really perfect as it requires a single responsible person having that account
though.

If none of the authors want to register a Client-ID for this fork, it would probably the best to just
keep using the Client-ID from livestreamer which is already present in the code. As previously
mentioned, the patch fixes the Client-ID issue.

Ok, so it looks like PyCharm understands:

```python
from ._version import __version__
```

and it understands

```python
from ._timeouts import *
__all__ += _timeouts.__all__
```

(I guess probably it's just picking up on the first line there and ignoring the `__all__` part.)

What it doesn't understand is:
```python
for _symbol in _core.__all__:
    _value = getattr(_core, _symbol)
    if getattr(_value, "_hazmat", False):
        setattr(hazmat, _symbol, _value)
        hazmat.__all__.append(_symbol)
    else:
        globals()[_symbol] = _value
        __all__.append(_symbol)
del _symbol, _value
```

which... you know... fair enough, I guess. But this explains the whole pattern you see for `trio` and `trio.hazmat`.

And then `_core`'s exports are also a mess of runtime generated stuff, with like helper decorators, and there's even some use of `eval`. (I swear it's the right thing in this particular weird case but... not so helpful here.)

It wouldn't be too bad to switch to keeping an explicit list of items that get imported into `trio`, `trio.hazmat`, and `trio.testing` from `trio._core`. The main reason we currently do it the way we do now is to make sure we don't accidentally provide some API in `trio._core` that we don't also provide to end-users somewhere ("keeping us honest"). But we could enforce that with a test in the test suite instead.

The big question is how we can express that list in a way that PyCharm understands, given that the things we're importing are not ultimately visible to it.

If you make an empty module with `__all__ = ["asdf"]` and then *don't define `asdf`*, does PyCharm offer `asdf` as a completion for that module?

What about a module with `from ._nonexistent import asdf`, where `_nonexistent.py` is, well, nonexistent. Does PyCharm offer `asdf` as a completion for that module?

@max-wittig sorry for the late answer.

So this is actually not a bug but a feature: http://python-gitlab.readthedocs.io/en/stable/release_notes.html#changes-from-1-0-2-to-1-1

This is a choice I made to avoid dealing with some issues: the objects now strictly reflect the GitLab API. So `GroupProject` doesn't implement the same features than `Project`. If you need to get issues related to a `GroupProject`, you have to create a `Project` object first:

```python
group_project = group.projects.list()[0]
issues = gl.projects.get(group_project.id, lazy=True).issues.list()
```

Hope this helps.

Sorry for this really late answer but I see the problem now: you need to use `https` in the URL, not `http`.

This is an HTTP protocol "feature": when POST request gets a 3xx answer (redirect), the new request is sent without data/body.

Update the URL to `https://gitlab.com` and you should be fine.
I'm closing this issue since this is a GitLab problem. Feel free to reopen if needed when GitLab provides a fix.

So the problem is that you are using `GroupProject` objects as `Project` objects, this doesn't work anymore (since v1.1). You need to do something like this (untested but should work):

```python
groups = gl.groups.list(search='my-group')
projects = groups[0].projects.list(all=True)
for p in projects:
    # create a lazy Project object; it's enough to get the `hook` manager
    p = gl.projects.get(p.id, lazy=True)
    for hook in p.hooks.list():
```

I think for this effort to be well adopted you need a bit more consideration and much larger conversation in the community about it. My personal opinion is that in an actual standard/interface we should not have things like Elu, Relu, LeakyRelu, ParametricRelu, MagicRelu, Selu, WhatNotRelu... If you really want to put forward this as a standard for portability definitely such fine grained operators are highly redundant. This can be seen from any of the graph based frameworks where this operator does not exist as a Graph Op but as the composition of those. Similarly, follows for reduction with associative operators. The whole idea of a standard I think has been going around for quite a while, but that must be quite general.

In my opinion, such interop is only possible if it goes only on the mathematical level, essentially having a mathematical description of programs which is agnostic to any implementation.

Here's a new idea that may help: what about moving all of SBProfile into the ```galsim::``` namespace (and similarly, wrapping directly it into the Python galsim package).  We've already agreed that SBProfile would be the core of GalSim, and if we have both ```galsim::``` and ```sbp::``` namespaces we'll always have trouble deciding where to put general-purpose C++ stuff.

I don't think hsm should go in the same namespace, but I think this would make it matter less whether it goes in ```galsim::hsm::``` or simply ```hsm::``` (I would certainly care less).  And if we do go with the latter, I think it's reasonable to do the same in Python, and make an eventual ```hsm``` Python package outside the galsim package - the motivation for keeping namespaces short in C++ applies to Python too, so if we like that motivation we can be consistent about liking it.  But moving sbprofile into a separate Python package outside galsim would have been problematic for dependency reasons, whereas hsm would not be.  And moving all of sbprofile into galsim solves that.
